author: @MrBudCole
text: Listen - I'm an old guy who read Marvel Comics for DECADES. When the MCU began with Iron Man, and Captain America - I was so excited, especially for Stan Lee. There were a few disasters that preceded these efforts, particularly the Fantastic Four and Incredible Hulk debacles. But, once they established Robert Downey, Jr, and Chris Evans as the NEW generation, I was hooked. Hemsworth as Thor also worked out pretty well, as did Johannson as Black Widow. Black Panther was decent, and the Guardians was SAVED by Pratt's performance. The Spiderman reboot (V3) was just OK. Then, for whatever reason, they decided to just screw everything up. They changed Captain Marvel into a woman - with no background explanation. They turned HULK into a scientist who was embarrassed by his own powers. Then they killed off their two top-performers - IRON MAN, and CAPTAIN AMERICA... the literal FOUNDATIONS for the empire they had built. They tried to replace them with likeable, but FAR LESS effective characters like Falcon, Wanda and Bucky. People checked them out, then said "nah - End Game was exactly what it said it was!" DC never did this with Superman. He never RETIRED, and gave his cape to someone else. His power was the fact that he was almost INVINCIBLE, and that's what attracted viewers. Same thing with Captain, and - even though his was personally flawed - Iron Man. Then they tried the She-Hulk, which was NEVER popular as a comic book, and was even LESS popular as a film. It's like this, folks. Superman is superhuman! He isn't political! He is JUST, even when it's uncomfortable. HULK is indestructible, the way he was originally written. He just gets stronger the more angry he becomes. No JUSTICE - just FORCE! When you put a cardigan sweater on him, and have him share some tacos - you have completely lost the entire reason that people want to watch a character that is completely IMPOSSIBLE in reality. WE don't want to see Hulk collecting FOOD for the needy! We want to see him SMASH whatever needs to be SMASHED! It's FICTION.

replies.author: @SageRhyme
replies.text:
While I don't disagree with your sentiment; the comics always hand off the capes and cowls to others, and Captain Marvel made the switch to Carol Danvers IN the comics because of Wonder Man.  Wonder Man and Captain Marvel were basically the same dude....  Wonder Man is coming in the MCU soon.

The problem wasn't handing Captain America off to another person, they did that in the comics.  It became Bucky, it also became the falcon.  The problem was that Falcon in the MCU turned down the super soldier serum, which makes no sense.  You can't be the captain without it.  Bucky has it.  Falcon could have had it.  You can't convince me the falcon could fight any hulk as just a normie.  

I'm fine with them mixing it up, but the mix ups need to make sense, and these mixups are just messups.  Also; Wanda isn't less effective, she's just not a hero, she's a villain.  They keep taking villains and somehow trying to wash their sins and make them "cool" because, well -- just because.  There is no developmental archs, you see Wanda enslave a bunch of people and then we're just supposed to forget she did that?  Everybody still loves her, like she's not a bad person?

I like how we got US Agent, and The Winter Soldier, and The Red Guardian and The Falcon, all of these are different versions of the Captain; which were all present in the comics... the thing is, they're all super soldiers in the comics, but not here in the MCU.  This is the big mistake.  They are fighting to make every villain a hero, and they are running out of bad guys.  That's the real problem.  In end game, we knew what the stakes were; there just are no stakes now.  They were supposed to be building to Galactus, then they pivoted, then it was Kang the Conqueror,  Jonathon Majors had a thing that he ended up proving was false, but they knee jerked and pivoted again, then it was secret wars, then they pivoted again....

The thing that made the MCU work wasn't Downey, wasn't Evan's -- it was the continuity with a final film in mind.  The continuity makes no sense now, and nobody can see where they are going, because they don't know where they are going....  It's ironically turned INTO the DCU, in that -- those movies only failed because they lacked vision, Marvel always had it.  They rush to bring us a million Marvels, instead of letting it cook.  Is Iron heart a thing?  What about Moon Knight; because dang Oscar Isaacs killed it, the fans loved it -- then they canceled season 2, and then said he wouldn't be a major character but he'd show up somewhere sometime.... LIKE HE'S LITERALLY AN EGYPTIAN ELDER GOD, he's not a street level dude like Dare Devil.  ALSO the best marvel show was the netflix DD/Punisher, and they're not fully utilizing those guys?  WHERE THE FUCK IS THE DAREDEVIL MOVIE WITH THE SPIDERMAN AND PUNISHER TEAM UP AGAINST KING PIN? 

Did they forget about Shang Chi?  Where the fuck is Blade -- and how do you stitch these things together in a way that makes sense, when they just went a fucking million directions at once.  The eternals has nothing to do with anything in the MCU, but somehow this is an MCU movie?  HOKAY.  Look I'm not mad they made Task Master a woman; I'm big mad that task master is on the team with Bucky Barnes now....  STOP IT.  Those after credits scenes were the stitch, to show you how the next movies were going to go.. They did it with Thor, but then he was absent in the next Guardians movie... what?

Who's driving drunk at marvel?

replies.author: @Wolf_ManJack
replies.text:
This is why it's actually almost funny when people say, "It's not the diversity, CHUDS, it's the *writing* at fault!"

The diversity mindset _completely informs_ the creative process these people allow themselves to participate in. It negates options, forces the inclusion of new characters, defines how sympathetic those characters must be, negotiates what political themes are allowed.

Yes, it is the writing; specifically, it is what intersectional diversity demands be *done to* the writing, and that is why the push for diversity is still guilty of creating these outcomes.

author: @Cinematic_Mahira
text: The single largest issue is the production methods. The way they make marvel films is not conducive to making good movies. CGI is rendered before a script is written to have big set pieces predetermined. Scripts are written quickly and not redrafted due to a mix of laziness and lack of time. Reshoots are presumed and often relied upon which balloons cost and can lead to disjointed scenes. Actors don’t care because it’s viewed as just a thing to get the next thing or make a big pay check. Directors and writers are casted for how they look rather than their work and skills. Example, Nia DeCosta was not prepared to direct a 200+ million movie. There are some other things but it’s in my opinion mainly that they have a production process that makes it extremely difficult to make quality films.

replies.author: @kapteeniratto
replies.text: Also another related thing on CGI: Marvels uses directors who have no experience with CGI. A directors who is good with CGI, can understand how the effects are gonna look from very raw, baseline sketch the effect team makes. The directors who don´t understand it, require a much more detailed sketches which adds pressure to a already tight schedule for the effects team. No wonder there has been a lot of bad CGI in resent Marvel movies.

author: @vmoutsop
text:
OK, I just looked this up:

* There are 1,971 movie theaters in the US at an "average" of a 164 seats per theater. 
* That's 323,244 seats. 
* A 2 hour movie can play 5-7 times a day. 
* In Boston, Captain America is playing on an average of 2-3 screens with 4-6 showings across 2-3 screens per day. 
* Avg ticket price is close to $20, not including refreshments. 

Using these averages and every seat is sold at $20, across 5 screens, it appears that any movie could only make a total of $96m per day or $290m per weekend total in the US. 

OK, I checked AMC theaters in Boston:

- 59 total seats sold for 1 showing at either 9 or 10pm.
- This was across 10 theaters in the metro Boston area
- Total of 30 screens for the show time.

Total $ for tickets = $1,180.00 @ $20 a ticket for the showing.

These numbers are probably not reflective of actual attendance across the country, but some of these box office numbers that are reported on doesn't seem possible or realistic.

This means that CA:BNW grossed just over half of the first film (adjusted for inflation), which was released before the MCU craze had ramped up, and over $1 billion less than the last CA film (again, adjusted for inflation). There is NO WAY that this film can be seen as anything but a massive failure.

author: @Jim_Bohner
text: There is no such thing as superhero fatigue, it's cinematic universe fatigue. Bad movies are compounding the issue but that issue is cinematic universes. Even if every movie was a banger you'd still see dwindling audiences because no one wants to have to watch countless TV shows just to know what's going on in the movie. If you went back to good, but more importantly, stand alone superhero movies you'd see audiences return. Not in the same numbers as Phase 1&2 but higher than recently.

Frankly, this is the reason why I never got all that much into the superhero comics when I was a kid, either -- I got tired of having to try to follow a half-a-dozen different titles I wasn't really interested in, in order to keep up with the storyline in the title I *was* interested in.
